story:
Loog is at it again. I keep wanting to call him something fancy like Leonardo, but my mouth won’t do it, and he hasn’t invented politeness yet anyway. He’s never satisfied with what the rest of us are—always poking at the world to make it do new tricks. Like when he made fire.

He watched lightning burn up everything and decided he’d be Mr. Lightning God in our cave. He rubbed sticks, whacked rocks, and stuffed lizards and hair in the pile because early science is messy. When it finally lit, I was dazzled by the glowing end of a stick and turned it toward Boog. Boog shooed it away with a sharp “OOG!” and somehow the ember found Loog’s eye. That’s why he’s one-eyed now, and why he had to invent an eye patch even though pirates haven’t been invented yet.

We’re used to fire now—nice pit, upright log seats, saber-toothed emu on sticks—though talking about it comes out mostly “Ooga ooo.” Loog wasn’t always mad at me. I learned the sling pretty well. The boomerang was a disaster—it always curved back to whack Loog, who invented the darn thing. Then came the bow: he strung tree limbs with vines and woolly aardvark gut, notched little spears, and while I was “helping,” an arrow went off and stuck in his behind. Hunting with clubs and hand-thrown spears hadn’t been feeding us great, but this was a mixed improvement.

Next, the wheel. Loog kept whonking rocks and failing with kind-of-square and triangular versions, then chasing us with his stone hammer when they didn’t roll right. Full of emu nuggets, I stood and stretched; a round section of log slipped free and rolled downhill. Who knew it would fly over a bump and smack Loog, perfect as a wheel and terrible as a helmet? He yelled “OOO! OOO!” which is as close to wheel as our language gets, right after he remembered how to breathe. He really needs a concussion protocol, but it’s hard to ask date, day, or President when none of those are invented.

The surprise party came before birthdays. We roasted drummies, hung flowers (aardvark innards got stinky), and hid with grape water gone bad and mushrooms. Boog wandered in; we jumped out yelling “ARRGHH!” He panicked and swung his club. Another concussion. Loog blamed us, naturally.

Then his potion phase. He was sure he could make Boog smarter faster than evolution. Lots of jars and pouring and math where he forgot to carry the seven. The result made Boog dimmer. Even the monkeys refused to try a sip after that. Loog decided he needed a test subject he could build.

Inspired by my cave drawings, he assembled a Loogenstein—pieces tied on a platform with vines. Graveyards and refrigeration would’ve helped, but we had vultures and heat. He pantomimed us up two spindly palms to hoist the platform into a storm. The sky went full Rolling Stones. A bolt split: half fried Boog, half zapped me. We let go; platform, monster, and both of us hit the ground together. Early gravity, you’re welcome. Loog had picked the exact spot under the falling everything and got stuck there for three days while we healed and forgot to miss him.

Which is why the wheel matters. If we’d thought to hang a couple in the trees with vines wrapped around them, we could’ve lifted that monster from the ground like geniuses. But we hadn’t invented that trick yet—our wheel only existed because my accidental rolling log had cracked Loog’s skull. He’s stomping and throwing things. I’ll dodge him till he cools off and paint our wall about the day Loog invented the wheel—Ooga, ooog ahh OOG ahhhh.
